speaker: interviewer
text: Singer in London, England. You talked about your intelligence work and other ways you've earned your living in this country. Was there anything else that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: In addition to my normal work, paid work, I collected words in different languages which the equivalent in particular in technical they're not known. And I found it hampered me in my work, so I decided to compile dictionaries, multi-lingual dictionaries, in different fields which I was working. First, I had-- I started with an astronomical dictionary in English, Russian, Chinese, and English. Then I saw that it, it was necessary to-- others asked me, particularly the Library of Congress of America asked me, the American Air Force. And that from different sides they asked me to help them with terminology. So I decided to make, compile, compiled a multi-lingual dictionary all in Russian, English, French, and German. You could-- and a special system which I had, you could translate from one in the other. If you had the German word, you could find the same equivalent in French, or in Russian, or in English. Had the French word, you could from the other three. And I made similar dictionaries. I made constructional engineering, kinematics. A few dictionaries. And collaborated to other dictionaries which were collected for institutes in America mostly, universities. I worked on some edited abstract journals in my new field which was cybernetics. It was automation and computer science. It was in the beginnings. And I brought in bioengineerings, different operators which I'm glad helped develop in medicine in different parts. I was glad that I could pay back to humanity what humanity gave me as a free person. And I still hear from my parents-- I was always told not to make distinction of race, color, religion. I continue to this day with the help of my wife and my children also goes this way. That helps me continue so. For me, a Jew is a Jew whatever he is. And reform or religious or-- I want to ask only to be a human being and behaving like such. And I hope it will go on like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lothar Singer, thank you very much for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lothar, tell us who is sitting next to you, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my partner and wife and lover, 50 years nearly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly, it will be in June. I was very fortunate that Lothar ended up and that he was where I was before him. And in the begin, [LAUGHS] he was a small dictator. But I managed to [LAUGHS] adjust him to the way like I felt. And afterwards, we got on very well. And we married. Nearly 50 years together. No complaints. I consider myself very lucky that I ended up with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your life in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's lovely. We really have a peaceful, a happy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to add as a statement at the end of this--

speaker: interviewee
text: The only--

speaker: interviewer
text: --record.

speaker: interviewee
text: --thing is to be aware and to be together. This is our main wish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to add anything, Lothar?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife is a work alcoholic.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Complains? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she always finds work. Not only does she knows that love goes through the tummy, produces only the best food for me and for our children and our guests, she always tries to help others-- is always busy working for other people-- charity work, and cultural field, and cultured society. And she has the same interpretation of life-- to help other people, to do what you can do today, or better said, yesterday, not to leave it for tomorrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm really thrilled with all the compliments. And we'll carry on as long as we are aware to do good work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paula and Lothar--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --thank you very much, indeed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a view of my hometown, Chernovitz, at approximately 1860. In the front, we can see a group of Jewish people discussing. Jews got known in the town from 1480.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my parents-- father, Schulim Singer, mother, Marjem Singer-- photographed about 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was after they were married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this person?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is myself at the age of one, taken in 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: On any special occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably a birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: A birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in a studio, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and what a lovely baby I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is myself at the age of four preparing for my later career in bobsledding.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four. This is a photo of myself in school uniform. There's a number on my left arm. My cousin Freddie Manfred Kimmel, who died during the Holocaust in the [? Arbeitsdorf ?] concentration camp. The little girl is my sister Ruth, at present living [? Kiryat Yam, ?] Israel. This is a photo of my grandfather-- the very left-- and his three brothers on the occasion of her wedding in about 1926. This is a photo of my class before the Baccalaureate in 1938, May. Unfortunately, I am the only survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: The third from the left. This is myself in 1938 in military uniform of the Romanian cadet.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18, just over 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any special reason it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember what the occasions was-- probably all were taken, separately for a photo. The building is housing the headquarters, the Jewish community of Chernovitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which building?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the right, the building, the larger building. In the back is-- you can still see the building of the Jewish school that my mother and grandfather had built.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is in what part of Chernovitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is on front of the theater, local theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a Russian postcard?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a Russian contemporary card, postcard.

speaker: interviewer
text: From?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it through the last relative which lived in the town, lived, left. This is myself, Sergeant Major Lothar Schulimovich Singer on the front in Poland, [? January ?] 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: '45. Sorry. Oh. This is a photo. It's the end of the war, approximately June 1945, made in Germany. I'm here in the Red Army officer uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any special reason for this photograph? Was it to send home or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, yes. These are the boys and the girls of my early training group, [INAUDIBLE] Romania, 1946, August.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Romanian in that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some were from Bessarabia. Most are here, Holocaust survivors. This is a photo of the leadership of Camp 61 of Jewish illegally immigrant made in Caraolos, near Famagusta, Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: When approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [INAUDIBLE] 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of mix were they in nationality terms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, here, there's Hungarian. And this is Polish. That's me here, there to the right. They're mixed-- Bulgarian. It's all a mixture. All are Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any from Palestine direct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, here is not direct. What a lovely pair-- myself and my bride, 22 April, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were each of you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 27 myself, and [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Paula, 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult getting married in a detention camp in Cyprus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not. We didn't have-- you got the permission from the Rabbitical Beth Din and from the camp leader, in this case myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the British?

speaker: interviewee
text: The British had nothing to do with this. Yes, we had even a wedding table. Here are all our relatives which I had in the camp. They were all illegal immigrants on the way to Israel. Rosh Hashanah card from the barbed wire camp in [INAUDIBLE] Cyprus. We are-- our photos are in the corner. Here is our camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you have sent this card to people outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult getting hold of the film or the print?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not. We had one which got illegal ways. He got in the fortress. This is also a New Year's card sent from the camps in Cyprian, Cyprus. Here you can see, also, our synagogue made from tent. These are the photos of my sister, Ruth, and my mother, Marjem Singer, after their return from concentration camp to Romania, [INAUDIBLE] taken [INAUDIBLE] in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: The grave stone of my late father, Schulim Singer, in [INAUDIBLE] Romania. Were you able to put that up straight away when he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not. I wasn't there. My late mom and my sister put it on. I was already in Cyprus, internee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately after he died in 24 December, 19-- a few months after his death on 21 of December, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a relative of my brother-in-law who was an architect and got the permission for repairs, and he put candles there. This is a maapilim ship, Pan-York, on board of which we came from Bulgaria to Cyprus, 19-- December, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was this photgraph taken, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken when the ship was released by the British authority and brought to Haifa a few years later. This is a photo album given by the group [INAUDIBLE] interned in Camp 61 in Cyprus on the occasion of our wedding, April, 1948. You can see that is the symbol-- the barbed wire, the ship, and the name in Hebrew of Cyprus, [? Kahfreseen. ?] The proud parents, Lothar and Paula, with their two sons, Selwyn and Mervyn. Selwyn, three years old, Mervyn, one year, taken in London, April, 1958.

speaker: interviewer
text: For any special reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, '59. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Any special reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The happy couple on the occasion of their visit to Paris for this wedding anniversary. Photo taken by my youth friend [INAUDIBLE] Monin [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] May, 1940-- '88. A legalized copy of my baccalaureate diploma made after the war in Bucharest as my original document had destroyed by the Nazis in Autachi, Bessarabia. Copy of my citation of the order by Stalin to my unit and myself on the occasion of the preparation for the offensive from the bridgehead Sandomierz on the Vistula.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have more than one of these?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a few ones, different occasion. For everybody-- for everyone, I got a medal, so I had about 18. Citation of the entire Red Army by the Commander in Chief, Joseph Vissarionovich Stalin given to my unit and myself for conquest of Dresden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name is written there in Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you read it to us in Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Lothar Schulimovich Singer. Document certifying the participation of accountant Singer Lothar Schulimovich in the great fatherland war. This is a copy of the internation order given by Lord Devinsta, Governor of Cyprus, for the internment of the members of the crew and passengers declared illegal Jewish immigrants from Cyprus on the second of January, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you say was interesting about this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was given after we were interned already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because if they've done it before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then it would have been legal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Illegal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Legal. After they've done this, you see, after you have put somebody in prison, you give the order to arrest him. Certificate that I have given the oath of allegiance in the [INAUDIBLE] in Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18 of tens, 1948. This is the ketubah given to us on the occasion of our wedding in Cyprus, 61 Camp. The left, you'll see the original stamp of the Beth Din of [? Kafrasin. ?] This is our ratification of our wedding certificate by the Beth Din of Haifa on February, in February, 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a given because the military authorities did not want to recognize our original certificate. These are various documents issued in Cyprus. One is on the occasions that the closure of the camp that I was 9 months the leader of the Camp 61. And the other is my permit by the English authority to leave the camp without escort to go to the HQ. And the other is this a permanent pass to-- from the Jewish authority to leave the camp. And the other, our identity papers in the camp. This is a copy of my original birth certificate on-- given on 17 July, 1946. The original was taken by the Nazis in Autachi, Bessarabia. Group of former Soviet officers leading the parade near the synagogue in London. I am the second from the left. Second from the right is Harold of the Soviet Union. This are some of my Russian-English-German-French dictionaries compiled by me. It's a byproduct of my work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many did you do together? Dictionaries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six or seven.
